coredata.dc:title: Sustainable synthesis of nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon quantum dots derived from Cissus quadrangularis for biomarker applications
coredata.dc:creator: Kansay, Vishal
Sharma, Varun Dutt
Chandan, G.
Sharma, Indu
Chakrabarti, S.
Bera, M.K.
coredata.dc:description: This study demonstrated an environmentally friendly, cost-effective, and easy method for manufacturing biocompatible fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from the leaf extract of the medicinal herb Cissus quadrangularis. As-synthesized nitrogen doped-CQDs had fluorescence quantum yields of up to 5%. The presence of pyrrolic/pyridinic-N, quaternary-N (N–C3), and OC–N, as verified by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, reveals that nitrogen atoms were efficiently doped in CQDs. The size distribution of as-synthesized CQDs was found to be within 4–9 nm using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope. Meanwhile, nuclear magnetic resonance studies showed the presence of a significant proportion of sp2 and sp3 carbon groups. The zeta potential of as-synthesized CQDs was −7.8 mV, demonstrating the presence of a negatively charged surface with good stability in aqueous solution. Furthermore, the development of easy, affordable, and non-destructive fluorescence-based staining agents as an alternative to organic or synthetic dyes is widely sought. In this regard, as-synthesized N-doped CQDs shown remarkable fluorescent staining capabilities in this study and might be employed as a useful probe for optical and bio-imaging of bacteria, fungal, and plant cells. On the other hand, the cytotoxicity and cell viability of pine leaf, mushrooms, algae, fungus, and bacteria have also been examined. Even at higher doses (10.9 mg/ml), cell survival was demonstrated to be rather excellent and cytotoxicity was minimal for up to 4 days.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon quantum dots
Green synthesis
Fluorescent stain
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Cissus quadrangularis
Biocompatibility and cytotoxicity study of N-CQDs
Morphological, structural and physiochemical characteristics of CQDs
Application of Cissus quadrangularis derived N-CQDs as fluorescent probe for bioimaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphical representation of synthesis of Cissus quadrangularis derived CQDs.

(a)–(d) High-resolution TEM images of N-CQDs and histogram of particle distribution with a Gaussian curve fitted to the data. (e) X-ray energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDX). (f) SAED patterns of as-prepared N-CQDs. (g) X-ray diffraction pattern of as-synthesized N-CQDs (h) 1H NMR spectra and (i) 13C NMR spectra of N-CQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum and deconvoluted spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s of as-synthesized N-doped CQDs. (e) FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs.
The dispersibility of Cissus quadrangularis derived as-synthesized N-CQDs in aqueous solution is examined through (a) Zeta potential and (b) Tyndall effect at various N-CQDs concentrations.

A UV–vis absorption spectrum of as-synthesized N-CQDs. The effect of (a) process temperature variation and (b) process time variation on the UV–vis spectra of N-CQDs and the related band gap variation table incorporated.

Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs subjected to hydrothermal (a) process temperature (b) process time variations. (c) Photographs of as-synthesized CQDs at various process temperatures exposed to daylight and UV light (365 nm).

Bioimaging of various biomaterials with N-CQDs. Digital images of original biomaterials (a), (e), (i), (m) & (q). Fluorescent images under bright field, green filter and red filter of pine leaf (Pinus roxburghii) (b–d), mushroom (agaricus bisporus) (f–h), algae (funaria hygrometrica) (j–l), fungus (alternaria alternata) (n–p), bacteria (staphylococcus aureus) (r–t). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Cytotoxicity test of N-CQDs on (a) pine leaf with concentration ranging from ∼1.7 mg/ml to ∼10.9 mg/ml, (b) mushroom with concentration ranging from ∼1.7 mg/ml to ∼10.9 mg/ml, (c) algae with concentration ranging from ∼0.5 mg/ml to ∼3 mg/ml, (d) fungus with concentration ranging from ∼1.7 mg/ml to ∼5 mg/ml, (e) bacteria with concentration ranging from ∼1.7 mg/ml to ∼5 mg/ml for up to 4 days.
A comparison of the synthesis technique, quantum yield, and particle size of CQDs synthesized from diverse medicinal herbs/plants components.

In Vitro and in Vivo Bioimaging and Other Applications
efficient inhibition of human hepatocellular carcinoma cells
chemical and thermal stimulus pain in mice
highly sensitive detection of salazosulfapyridine in mouse plasma

head.ce:title: Sustainable synthesis of nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon quantum dots derived from Cissus quadrangularis for biomarker applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, MM Engineering College, Maharishi Markandeshwar (Deemed to be University), Mullana, Ambala, 133207, Haryana, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, MM Engineering College, Maharishi Markandeshwar (Deemed to be University), Mullana, Ambala, 133207, Haryana, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, M M Institute of Medical Sciences and Research, Maharishi Markandeshwar (Deemed to be University), Mullana, Ambala, 133207, Haryana, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: M M Institute of Medical Sciences and Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biochemistry, M M Institute of Medical Sciences and Research, Maharishi Markandeshwar (Deemed to be University), Mullana, Ambala, 133207, Haryana, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, MM Engineering College, Maharishi Markandeshwar (Deemed to be University), Mullana, Ambala, 133207, Haryana, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biotechnology, MM Engineering College, Maharishi Markandeshwar (Deemed to be University), Mullana, Ambala, 133207, Haryana, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study demonstrated an environmentally friendly, cost-effective, and easy method for manufacturing biocompatible fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from the leaf extract of the medicinal herb Cissus quadrangularis. As-synthesized nitrogen doped-CQDs had fluorescence quantum yields of up to 5%. The presence of pyrrolic/pyridinic-N, quaternary-N (N–C3), and OC–N, as verified by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, reveals that nitrogen atoms were efficiently doped in CQDs. The size distribution of as-synthesized CQDs was found to be within 4–9 nm using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope. Meanwhile, nuclear magnetic resonance studies showed the presence of a significant proportion of sp2 and sp3 carbon groups. The zeta potential of as-synthesized CQDs was −7.8 mV, demonstrating the presence of a negatively charged surface with good stability in aqueous solution. Furthermore, the development of easy, affordable, and non-destructive fluorescence-based staining agents as an alternative to organic or synthetic dyes is widely sought. In this regard, as-synthesized N-doped CQDs shown remarkable fluorescent staining capabilities in this study and might be employed as a useful probe for optical and bio-imaging of bacteria, fungal, and plant cells. On the other hand, the cytotoxicity and cell viability of pine leaf, mushrooms, algae, fungus, and bacteria have also been examined. Even at higher doses (10.9 mg/ml), cell survival was demonstrated to be rather excellent and cytotoxicity was minimal for up to 4 days.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanomaterials have sparked considerable interest in the twenty-first century for a wide range of applications, notably in the biomedical arena, including bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery, photodynamic treatment, and so on [1]. Fluorescent nanomaterials have attracted a lot of attention as prospective competitors to standard fluorescent dye probes in recent years, and they have evolved rather quickly due to the high demand for fluorescent probes in chemical sensing, biological monitoring, and other related applications. Bioimaging is a powerful technology for live cell imaging research that uses fluorescence microscopy and is used in many scientific areas [2]. It enables real-time observation of biological processes. In bioimaging research, a fluorescent probe is required to view the image of the cells/specimen species. Many commonly used organic fluorophores/dyes, such as rhodamine 6G, 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), hoechist-33258, hoechist-33342, acridine orange, ethidium bromide, hydroxycoumarin, alexa fluor 488, and Cy5, are commercially available with very high quantum yields of up to 80% [3–5]. However, there are several disadvantages with using organic fluorophores in bioimaging, such as their photobleaching susceptibility and ease of leaking out of the cytoplasm into media. They have reduced photostability, which means they deteriorate when exposed to light throughout the imaging process. They are also cytotoxic, have a limited wavelength range for excitation and emission, and so forth.

In comparison to organic fluorophores, metal-based chalcogenide inorganic semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) like CdX (X = Se, Te) capped with different long-chain alkyl thiols have been widely employed as fluorescent probes during the last decade [6]. The unique characteristics of semiconductor QDs over organic fluorophores, such as the quantum confinement effect, provide them an edge over organic fluorophores. That is, changing the particle size of QDs causes changes in optical characteristics (for example, increasing the particle size shifts the absorption in the longer wavelength owing to a decrease in bandgap energy), and one may alter the optical properties (absorption, emission wavelengths, and emission quantum yield) simply by adjusting the particle size during the synthesis process or post-synthesis treatments [7–9]. In addition, quantum dots are more photostable than organic fluorophores because of their ligand-coated core-shell structure [10]. Even though fluorescent nanoparticles like CdS, PbS quantum dots, copper or palladium nanoclusters are currently used extensively in a variety of scientific and technical fields, they have a disadvantage in terms of biocompatibility because the majority of QDs contain harmful heavy metals like Cd, Pb and Hg in their composition [11]. Hazardous metals that are toxic to living cells may escape from the cell during the imaging investigation, despite the fact that they were enfolded with surface coverage. Therefore, new fluorescent materials with better optical and biological characteristics for bioimaging of living cells should be developed in order to address the aforementioned problems of limited biodegradability and toxicity.

In this situation, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have shown promise as a cost-effective replacement for conventional materials in such vital fields. In terms of photobleaching resistance, chemical inertness, simplicity of surface functionalization, and other factors, carbon quantum dots may outperform traditional organic fluorophores and cutting-edge inorganic semiconductor QDs. Carbon quantum dots have demonstrated outstanding photo stability, tiny size, tightly regulated photoluminescence, biocompatibility, electrochemiluminescence, and astonishing multi-photon excitation (up-conversion) capacity since their surprising discovery in 2006 [12]. CQDs are appealing carriers for drug delivery, biological imaging, and other applications because they can be functionalized with biomolecules, are less toxic, and have inert chemical natures [13]. Despite the fact that semiconducting quantum dots are often made using affordable precursors, however, repeatability is still a problem [14]. Thus, it is crucial to develop and build a straightforward, economical, and eco-friendly method based on affordable precursors. When compared to common inorganic QDs, which can be produced using inexpensive methods, CQDs offer significant and distinctive characteristics such as excellent biocompatibility, specific biological target, minimal toxicity, and a substantial quantum size impact [15].

In the quest for an efficient, economical, and ecologically acceptable CQD synthesis process, natural biomaterial resources are frequently preferred over alternative organic, inorganic, or synthetic resources. This is largely due to biomaterials being renewable and biocompatible, as well as their ability to convert biomass waste into useable and valuable commodities [16]. Several investigations on the synthesis of carbon-based nanomaterials utilizing natural precursors such as orange juice, green tea, egg, potatoes, lotus root, pepper, coriander leaves, and other herbal medicinal plants have recently been reported [17,18]. Green synthesis methods, on the other hand, are far more acceptable than traditional physical and chemical procedures. A bottom-up hydrothermal approach that uses natural precursors has aroused a lot of interest due to its eco-friendliness, ease of usage, low cost, and mass manufacture.

One of the primary goals of this research is to design a simple and successful experimental technique for low-cost CQD manufacturing based on a one-step hydrothermal synthesis of a traditional medicinal herb extract popularly known as chardhara plant (Cissus quadrangularis). Cissus quadrangularis, a grape family plant, is high in vitamin C and antioxidants. It has been used for millennia to treat a range of health issues, and its extracts are now widely available as herbal supplements [19].

Fluorescent dyes, also known as fluorophores, enable researchers to employ fluorescence microscopy in fluorescent labeling of diverse living organisms such as bacteria, fungi, plant cells, and so on. However, for a sustainable future, it is vital to use biocompatible, non-toxic, ecologically acceptable, and cost-effective fluorescent materials. As a result of this research, we demonstrated the key material and optical properties of CQDs synthesized from Cissus quadrangularis leaf extract, as well as a fluorescent staining approach for employing those CQDs as fluorescent markers for bio-imaging.

Cissus quadrangularis popularly known as chardhara plant was harvested from the MMDU Mullana botanical garden which is located on campus (GPS locations: 30°24′ N, 77°04′ E) as well as neighboring locations for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots. Ethylene diamine (EDA) with a purity of 99.9% bought from Merck. Research grade solvents such as hexane, toluene, chloroform, acetonitrile, acetone, propan-1-ol, and methanol with 99.9% purity bought. All the chemicals were used as-received without further purification. An aqua regia solution (HCl: HNO3 = 3:1) was used to clean the glassware. A Milli-Q system was used to obtain deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) (Millipore, France).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Cissus quadrangularis
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs derived from Cissus quadrangularis were synthesized in the following way: Before crushing and grinding the Cissus quadrangularis plant into juice, it was carefully washed with distilled water. Following that, ethylenediamine (EDA) was added in a 7:3 ratio to the plant juice, and the mixture was ultrasonicated at room temperature for 15 min. EDA was deliberately used as a nitrogen source for CQD doping. After that, the mixture was put in a hydrothermal Teflon-lined autoclave for 3 h at temperatures ranging from 150 to 220 °C. To remove impurities, the material was centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm after cooling overnight. The residual contamination was then filtered via a 0.22-μm polytetrafluoroethylene syringe filter. Purified CQDs were then obtained, and under UV light (365 nm, 60 W), they showed a strong green-yellowish hue. The filtered solution was then freeze-dried in a lyophilizer at −40 °C and kept at 4 °C for future use. Fig. 1 shows a schematic representation of the whole synthesis process.

The relative values of quantum yield, QY (ɸ), for Cissus quadrangularis leaf extract CQDs were examined using quinine sulfate dye as a reference standard material. For this measurement, an aqueous solution of the required dye was produced and Equation (1) was used:
(1)
φ = φ
r
(
I
x
I r
ƞ x 2
ƞ r 2
)
(
A r
A x
)
where x represents the CQDs, r represents the standard dye i.e. quinine sulfate (QY: 0.54), I
r
and I
x represents the fluorescence emission intensity of reference and unknown sample, respectively. The optical absorption values of the reference and unknown samples are represented by A
r and A
x , respectively. Meanwhile, refractive index values given by η
r and η
x reflect the refractive index value of the reference and unknown materials, respectively.

The QY of CQDs was found to be around 5%. It is worth noting that as compared to organic or inorganic sources, the greater quantum yield of CQDs generated from diverse sections of medicinal herbs/plants is still a matter for worry. It is worth noting that as compared to organic or inorganic sources, the greater quantum yield of CQDs generated from diverse sections of medicinal herbs/plants is still a matter for worry. In this connection, Table 1 shows a comparison of quantum yield of CQDs synthesized from various medicinal plants/herbs based on the available data in the literature.

We used a range of resources for this investigation, including pine (Pinus roxburghii) plant leaves, mushrooms (Agaricus bisporus), algae (Funaria hygrometrica), fungus (Alternaria alternata), and bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus) for bioimaging [31]. Pine leaves and mushrooms were chopped into adequate numbers for slide processing using sharp blades. The sliced pieces float in the DI water. They are carefully removed with a brush and placed in a watch glass filled with DI water. Thin slices are removed using a brush and placed in a drop of water on a slide before being examined under an optical microscope. Following that, a thin, homogeneous slice of skin was stained. Fluorescent stains were then exclusively used to stain plant foliage and mushrooms. The section cutting was first kept on a glass slide for this reason. The pH was adjusted to ∼7.2 before applying the N-CQDs-based alternative fluorescent stain. Following that, a few drops of N-CQDs-based alternative fluorescent stain derived from Cissus quadrangularis (∼1 mg/ml) were employed to thoroughly submerge the section slices in the stain and covered with a coverslip before being investigated under a fluorescence microscope. Similarly, after dropping fluorescent N-CQDs (0.5 mg/ml) onto a glass slide, a very little tuft of algae or fungal mycelium containing spores was added, followed by a coverslip, and the slide was examined using a fluorescent microscope. The bacterial smear was stained with fluorescent N-CQDs (0.5 mg/ml) before air drying. A sliding rack was used to hold the smears in place. Afterwards, the fluorescent dye was gently applied to the smear.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and cytotoxicity study of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The intrinsic fluorescence of CQDs was used to investigate biocompatibility with a variety of materials including pine plant, mushroom, fungus, algae, and bacteria. The viability experiment was carried out over a period of three to four days with various concentrations. Prior to the studies, epidermal layers of pine and mushroom were deposited on a slide. The epidermal layers of pine and mushroom were then stained for 4 days with different concentrations of generated N-CQDs (∼1.7 mg/ml, ∼2.5 mg/ml, ∼5 mg/ml, and ∼10.9 mg/ml). Similarly, we test the survivability of algae, fungi, and bacteria at various doses (∼0.5 mg/ml, ∼1 mg/ml, and ∼3 mg/ml) for 4 days. As seen in the image, controls were those that did not have any stains [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological, structural and physiochemical characteristics of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological properties of the as-synthesized N-doped CQDs were examined using a high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). Fig. 2 a–d shows the TEM image of the as-synthesized N-CQDs with different scale of resolution. CQDs are found to be monodispersed, small, and almost spherical in shape. The particles range in size from 4 to 9 nm in diameter, with an average diameter of 5.1 nm (see inset of Fig. 2b), demonstrating the nanoscale of CQDs for their quantum confinement effect. The dispersibility of CQDs in water was shown to have a trend toward bigger aggregates, most likely due to the lack of a deliberate capping agent to prevent aggregation (see Fig. 2d). Moreover, the elemental composition of the as-synthesized N-CQDs was investigated by using energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). The presence of a large amount of carbon (48.37 wt%), nitrogen (10.39 wt%), and oxygen (41.24 wt%) concentrations is observed in the EDX spectra shown in Fig. 2e. Furthermore, the broad diffused ring in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern revealed that the as-synthesized N-CQDs had a poor poly-crystalline structure (Fig. 2f).

The structural properties of the as-synthesized N-CQDs were investigated using an x-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern, as shown in Fig. 2g, which verified the sample's poor crystallinity. The obtained broad diffraction peak with a center angle of 23.8 corresponds to the (002) plane, which was attributed to the interlayer packing of disordered carbon atoms in the hexagonal graphite structure of CQDs, producing a turbostratic structure (JCPDS No. 26–1076) [33]. The amorphous nature of the synthesized QDs also contributed to the wide peak. The presence of a weak peak at 42.1° indicates that graphitization has occurred along the (100) plane. The interlayer distance (d-spacing) for the (002) plane was determined to be 0.37 nm and for the (100) plane to be 0.21 nm [34,35].

Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, on the other hand, was used to investigate the composition of the core structure of CQDs, especially when paired with sp3 and sp2 hybridization. Indeed, the 1H and 1³C NMR of CQDs were used to differentiate between sp3-and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. The depicted 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. 2h) shows the existence of four separate chemical shift (δ) areas. 1–3 ppm (dominated by sp3 C–H protons), 3–6 ppm (associated with protons of hydroxyl, ether, and carbonyl groups), 6–8 ppm (for aromatic or sp2 protons), and 8–10 ppm (related to aldehydic protons) [36]. The figure illustrates that the first two regions indicated above surpass the final two, suggesting that sp3-hybridized carbon atoms dominate sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. Likewise, 1³C NMR spectra (Fig. 2i) also revealed a hybridization of sp³ and sp2 carbon atoms. It also demonstrates four distinct chemical shift (δ) zones i.e., 20–80 ppm (for sp3 carbons and carbons associated with hydroxyl and amine groups), 80–100 ppm (for carbons linked with ether linkages), 100–120 ppm (for CC aromatic or sp2 hybridized carbon atoms), and 175–190 ppm (for CO carbons) [37].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to further investigate the nature of nitrogen functional forms found in as-synthesized N-CQDs. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 3 a) shows a strong graphitic C1s peak at around 284.5 eV, a distinct N1s peak at 398.8 eV, and an O1s peak at 529.9 eV. The C1s signal was divided into three components, each with a centered energy of 282.7 eV, 283.9 eV, and 285.9 eV, representing sp2C-sp2C, N-sp2C, and C–OH bonds, respectively (see Fig. 3b) [38,39]. Aside from the well-known C–N bond obtained at (283.9 eV), the high-resolution spectra of N1s (Fig. 3c) can be deconvoluted into three distinct peaks, each centered on 397.2 eV, 397.7 eV, and 398.8 eV, respectively as a result of pyrrolic/pyridinic-N, quaternary-N (N–C3), and OC–N, demonstrating that nitrogen atoms were effectively doped in CQDs [40]. Furthermore, the O1s spectra shown in Fig. 3d can be divided into two components, 528.8 eV and 529.9 eV, indicating the presence of the C= O and C–OH/C–O–C groups.

FTIR investigations on carbon quantum dots (CQD) verified the presence of multiple nitrogen and oxygen functional groups as well as linkages, as shown in Fig. 3e. Stretching frequencies of 3334.39, 2952.51, 1624.45, 1356.56, and 923.37 cm−1 firmly corroborated the existence of –OH, –C–H, CO, O–H, and C–N, respectively [41]. The presence of several functionalization groups (such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, amine, and carboxyl groups) on the surface of N-CQDs is therefore suggested by FTIR spectra. Furthermore, the presence of these hydrophilic functional groups is anticipated to improve the water solubility of carbon quantum dots.

Meanwhile, Zetasizer was utilized to determine the surface charge of CQDs, also known as the electro kinetic potential of the synthesized fluorescent CQDs. This approach further highlights CQD features such as their double layer structure and range of hydrophilic or hydrophobic functional groups. The zeta potential of nanoparticles in solution is affected by their surface charge. It's a useful way to figure out the surface condition of nanoparticles as well as the long-term stability of a colloidal dispersion. The stability of CQD particles over short and long time periods determines the magnitude of Zeta potential. A measured Zeta potential of −7.8 mV as shown in Fig. 4 a demonstrates that CQDs without surface passivation are reasonably stable in aqueous solution. Furthermore, at room temperature, the aqueous solution of N-CQDs showed a long-term homogenous phase with no apparent precipitation. This was confirmed further by applying the Tyndall effect, the most widely used technique for particle dispersibility in a colloid, and leveraging the phenomenon of light scattering in colloidal dispersion while exhibiting no light in a real solution [42]. To carry out the Tyndall experiment, a colloidal solution of as-synthesized N-CQDs was irradiated with a red laser beam (650 nm, 5 mW). As a result of it, individual suspended CQDs scatter and reflect light, allowing the laser beam to be seen as it travels through colloidal solutions, but non-colloidal DI water remains clear due to the Tyndall effect, as seen in Fig. 4b. For this investigation, different concentrations of CQDs were used. In general, as seen in Fig. 4b, the dispersion of light rises as the concentration of N-CQDs increases.

Fluorescent CQDs demonstrated high optical absorption in the UV region, with a tail extending into the NIR. It was revealed that optical absorption is hydrothermal process condition dependent, i.e. process temperature and duration. As shown in Fig. 5 a, CQDs displayed three absorbance peaks centered at 572 nm, 479 nm, and 390 nm at a lower processing temperature (150 °C). The low-intensity absorption peak at 572 nm is attributed to the lower content and weak electron transition from n–π* due to the presence of functional groups such as –NH2 or –OH, while the peak at 479 nm is attributed to the n→π* transition due to the presence of CO and CN in the core and the highest intensity absorption is caused by the π–π* transition due to the presence of CC bond in the core [43]. Meanwhile, when the temperature of the hydrothermal process rises, the low-intensity absorption peak disappears, and the whole absorption peaks move to lower wavelengths. In fact, for process temperature ranges of 180–200 °C (Fig. 5a), the peak centered sifted to ∼484 nm and ∼372 nm, respectively, assigned to n→π* transition owing to the existence of CO and CN and π–π* transition due to the presence of CC bond in the core [44]. However, when the process temperature increased to 220 °C, the absorption peak shifted back to higher wavelengths, with peaks centered around 537 nm and 384 nm.

This is believed to be due to the addition of graphitic nitrogen to the carbon sp2 lattice because the graphitic nitrogen centers can inject excess electrons into the vacant π* orbitals, significantly reducing the HOMO-LUMO gap and the energies of the relevant optical transitions [45,46]. Meanwhile, O-containing functional groups (such as hydroxyl, carboxyl) on the surface of CQDs may restrict the energy levels, resulting in red-shifted absorption [44,47,48]. A similar tendency for process time change was seen for a specific process temperature (180 °C) while maintaining the process temperature constant, as illustrated in Fig. 5b. A significant shift in absorption peaks during a process length of 720 min is most likely caused by the greater content of functional groups owing to EDA-carbon precursor interaction. On the other hand, the band gap energy of CQDs was derived from UV data (Fig. 5a) using the Tauc plot for direct band gap suing the equation
(
α h υ
)
2
= A
(
h υ −
E g
)
, where α, h, A, and E
g are the absorption coefficient, light frequency, blank constant, constant, and band gap energy, respectively [49]. The details of the computation may be found in the supplemental materials (Fig. S1 and Fig. S2). Due to process variations, a band gap in the range of 2.6–2.9 eV was observed (the value is tabulated inside Fig. 5a and b).

In contrast, carbon quantum dots are characterized by their size-dependent fluorescence activity and emission wavelength. Hence, the process parameter dependent fluorescence emission of CQDs was studied. The fluorescence spectra (FL) of CQDs dispersed in water are shown in Fig. 6 a, and the FL emission spectra of CQDs was discovered to be hydrothermally process dependent. The FL emission spectra showed a broader asymmetric peak range from 380 to 513 nm following excitation with a wavelength of 280 nm as the process temperature increased from 150 to 220 °C. The deconvoluted fluorescent spectrum as shown in Supplementary Fig. S3 revealed three distinct broad peaks for the process temperatures of 150 and 180 °C. As the process temperature increased from 150 to 180 °C, the main peak shifted towards lower wavelength from its center at 505 nm (Areal contribution: 59.16%, FWHM: 153.4 nm) to 474 nm (areal contribution: 44.94%, FWHM: 101.1 nm), while the middle peak shifted towards higher wavelength from its center at 481 nm (areal contribution: 40.19%, FWHM: 69.7 nm) to 515 nm (areal contribution: 36.32%, FWHM: 179.7 nm). Meanwhile, the shoulder peaks also shifted towards higher wavelength from its center at 380 nm (areal contribution: 6.32%, FWHM: 83.9 nm) to 386 nm (areal contribution: 18.72%, FWHM: 97.1 nm). Similarly, when the process temperature raised up to 220 °C, all of the peaks migrated towards lower wavelengths, as seen in Fig. 6a and Supplemental Fig. S3. The asymmetric widening of FL spectra was also observed in normalized FL spectra where each FL data was divided by its maximum intensity, as can be seen from Supplemental Fig. S4a. Therefore, this temperature-dependent widening of FL spectra clearly demonstrates multi-color fluorescence emission, which is apparently produced by a rise in the degree of carbonization and shape modification of CQDs as process temperature increases.

Similarly, the influence of various duration on FL emission was examined for a certain process temperature. The FL emission spectra broadened asymmetrically following excitation with a 280 nm wavelength when the process time was held constant at 3 h, as seen in Fig. 6b and the normalized FL spectra in Supplementary Fig. S4b, but the emission peak is reduced for shorter (1.5 h) and longer (12 h) durations. Again, the deconvoluted fluorescent spectrum as shown in Supplementary Figs. S5a–c revealed three distinct broad peaks in general for various process durations, however, with a 3 h process duration, the (i) main contributed peak became wider and shifted towards shorter wavelength having peak center at 474 nm (areal contribution: 44.94%, FWHM: 101.1 nm), (ii) the medium contributed peak was shifted towards higher wavelength with peak center at 515 nm (areal contribution: 36.32%, FWHM: 179.7 nm), however, the (iii) lowest contributed peak was shifted towards shorter wavelength with peak center at 386 nm (areal contribution: 18.72%, FWHM: 97.1 nm) than the others.

It is worth mentioning that, N-CQDs synthesis processes were repeated multiple times for each processing temperature studied in the range of 150–220 °C and the relevant fluorescence characteristics were measured. Since FL emission is critical in this study, therefore, on the basis of its consistency and repeatability, the process temperature was optimized to be 180 °C for 3 h. As a consequence of this FL emission analysis, greenish-colored fluorescence emission seems to prevail. In fact, in the daylight, the aqueous solution of CQDs was pale yellow and clear, but when exposed to UV light (365 nm, 60 W), it fluoresced intensely and vividly green, as seen in Fig. 6c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Cissus quadrangularis derived N-CQDs as fluorescent probe for bioimaging
ce:section.ce:para: The goal of this research is to look towards alternative fluorescent stains or markers for living plant cells, tissues or organelles. Fluorescent dyes are a quick and easy way to measure cell viability and visualize organelle distribution and dynamics. Fluorescein isothiocyanate, Tetramethylrhodamine-5-(and 6)-isothiocyanate, fluorescein, coumarin, cyanine, rhodamine, DAPI (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole), tetramethylrosamine, and other widely used fluorescent staining agents have all been previously reported in the literature [50]. The manufacturer's recommended staining procedures, including the suitable carrier solvents, cell cultures medium, final concentrations, temperature adjustment etc. Must be followed when using such fluorescent dyes. Moreover, the fluorescent agents must be supplied as concentrated stocks in carrier solvents containing dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) or dimethyl formamide at the required concentrations in order to achieve final concentrations [50]. Fortunately, the lengthier procedures mentioned above are not necessary for the novel N-doped CQD-based fluorescent stain synthesized from Cissus quadrangularis in this study. Even though the general staining strategy was adequate for preliminary screening, an attempt was made to minimize the quantity of fluorescent stain necessary to stain a certain number of cells while simultaneously optimizing the overall staining procedure. This was chosen primarily to reduce the expense of labelling cells for field-scale research while also enabling for the staining process to be scaled up. Staining of stationary cultures, log-phase cultures, ambient temperature (32 °C) or cycling temperature ranges of 25–37 °C, and staining with different fluorescent stain concentrations (0.5 mg/ml to 10.9 mg/ml) were also performed. On average, fluorescent staining takes 2–4 h. A Zeiss Axio imager fluorescent microscope with a 100 Watt mercury lamp, an episcopic fluorescence attachment, an H-III photomicrographic attachment, and multiple color filters was used to assess the staining qualitatively.

N-CQDs derived from Cissus quadrangularis were employed as fluorescent markers in the bioimaging of diverse plant leaves, mushrooms, algae, fungus, and bacteria as illustrated in Fig. 7 . The white button mushroom (Agaricus bisporus) was used in this study. Similarly, pine leaves (Pinus roxburghii), algae (Funaria hygrometrica), fungus (Alternaria alternata), and bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus) were chosen for bioimaging studies. The pine leaf structure is clearly visible after staining (see Fig. 7a–d), with a single-layered, thick-walled epidermis covered by a thick cuticle and interrupted by depressed stomata over the surface (amphistomatic). Each stoma has two guard cells and two subsidiary cells. It opens into a vestibule cavity on the outside and a substomatal cavity on the inside. Under the epidermis, there are a few layers of thick sclerenchymatous hypodermis. There is no distinction between palisade and spongy parenchyma.

This region's cells are thin-walled, parenchymatous, polygonal, and compactly organized, with chloroplasts and starch grains. The inner surface develops peg-like infoldings. It is bordered by single-layered endodermis with barrel-shaped cells and casparian strips. Just under the endodermis is a multilayered pericycle with a T-shaped mass of sclerenchymatous cells sandwiched between two vascular bundles [51].

Typically, a mushroom consists of a stem, a cap, and gills. The gills of the mushroom are frequently inspected under a microscope to determine the mushroom classification [52]. The spores are the most significant microscopic trait for identifying mushrooms. The color, shape, size, attachment, and reactivity to chemical testing of mushroom spores typically leads to identification. After successfully staining with as-synthesized CQDs, the borders of the gills are clearly noticeable under the fluorescent microscope, forming the hymenial layer, or hymenium, which forms the spore-bearing structures known as basidia (see Fig. 7e–h). Many spores (basidiospores) can be seen on the surface of this layer or free between the gills.

The most prevalent algae species is Funaria hygrometrica. It grows in dense tufts on rocks, tree trunks, moist walls, and damp soils [53]. They aid in the production of soil (Pedogenesis). The presence of well-defined midribs in the algal structure is readily visible after staining with as-synthesized N-CQDs (see Fig. 7i-l). It contains multiple layers of cells, but the lateral ‘wing’ or lamina is only one layer of thin-walled, chloroplast-rich cells. Midrib is composed of short strands of slightly thickened thin cells that assist in conduction.

Alternaria alternate, on the other hand, is a widespread fungus species found in organic and water-rich settings (or moisture). It is often a saprophyte, although it may also be a necrotrophic plant pathogen on a variety of host plants [54]. The fungal cytoplasm is visible on the slide as a fluorescent color region containing hyphae, conidiophores, phialides, and conidia surrounded by fluorescent color (see Fig. 7m–p). Fluorescent microscope was also utilized to examine the bacterial shape and cell configurations. Staphylococcus aureus bacteria are spherical in shape. They are typically seen in the mucous membranes of the mouth and respiratory system, where they have been linked to a variety of illnesses and infections such as sepsis, pneumonia, and pharyngitis [55]. Staphylococcus aureus bacteria, like many other bacteria, are tiny, measuring between 0.5 and 2.0 μm in diameter. As a result of the low magnification, Staphylococcus aureus display a flashing of fluorescent green color emission when examined using a fluorescent microscope (see Fig. 7q–t).

In addition, the cytotoxicity and cell viability of pine leaf, mushrooms, algae, fungus, and bacteria have been examined, as shown in Fig. 8 . A cell viability of more than 80% is achieved, indicating that the CQDs are biocompatible and have low cytotoxicity [56]. Fig. 8a and b shows that N-CQDs had a low cytotoxicity for pine leaf and mushroom, respectively even at higher concentrations (10.9 mg/ml) for up to 4 days. Similarly, algae, fungi, and bacteria revealed minimal cytotoxicity for CQD doses up to 3 mg/ml when tested for 4 days, as shown in Fig. 8c–e.

However, the high emission quantum yield of carbon quantum dots remains a challenge, and further advancement is necessary before they can be widely used in bioimaging applications. Moreover, the defects in carbon quantum dots play a vital role in the fluorescence properties, which may contribute to enhance bioimaging sensitivity or selectivity.

In conclusion, fluorescent carbon quantum dots were synthesized from the medicinal plant Cissus quadrangularis as a green source in a one-step, easy, eco-friendly, and cost-effective way utilizing a hydrothermal assisted synthesis process. Initially, a hydrothermal process was carried out at temperatures ranging from 150 to 220 °C, and the relevant fluorescence properties were evaluated. Since fluorescence emission is crucial in this study, the process temperature was optimized to 180 °C for 3 h based on consistency and repeatability. The as-synthesized carbon quantum dots displayed high fluorescence intensity, outstanding photostability, and efficient multicolor fluorescent emission after UV irradiation. Furthermore, the as-synthesized CQD fluorescence quantum yield was around 5%. According to HRTEM and XRD investigations, as-synthesized CQDs demonstrated near-spherical shape with an average size of 5.1 nm and are a mixture of amorphous and polycrystalline in nature. NMR spectra revealed the presence of both sp2 and sp3 carbon groups in substantial levels. Furthermore, the as-synthesized CQDs displayed a strong water suspension capacity, with Zeta potential studies indicating a negative surface charge (−7.8 mV). Besides, CQDs derived from Cissus quadrangularis have been demonstrated to be an alternative fluorescent staining agent in bio-labeling of bacteria, fungi, and plant cells due to their simplicity, cheap cost, and green manufacturing. The cytotoxicity and cell viability of pine leaf, mushrooms, algae, fungus, and bacteria, on the other hand, have been investigated. Cell survival and cytotoxicity have been found to be acceptable even at higher dosages (10.9 mg/ml) for up to 4 days.
Not applicable.
Not applicable.
Not applicable.
Not applicable.
Not applicable.

Vishal Kansay: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Varun Dutt Sharma: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. G. Chandan: Investigation, Formal analysis. Indu Sharma: Investigation, Formal analysis. S. Chakrabarti: Methodology, Supervision. M.K. Bera: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, overall, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
